Following {{ill|World War II|ja|第二次世界大戦|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/World War II|qq=}}, its excellent {{ill|transportation access|ja|交通アクセス|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/transportation access|qq=}} facilitated rapid expansion, establishing it as one of the major onsen areas in the prefecture alongside {{ill|Kusatsu Onsen|ja|草津温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kusatsu Onsen|qq=}} and {{ill|Ikaho Onsen|ja|伊香保温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikaho Onsen|qq=}}. In the {{ill|Jomo Karuta|ja|上毛かるた|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jomo Karuta|qq=}}, it is celebrated as "Mi no fuda: Minakami-Tanigawa, skiing and climbing." == Hot Spring Properties == * Calcium - {{ill|Sulfate spring|ja|硫酸塩泉|1=Sulfate spring|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sulfate spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Sulfate spring|qq=}} == Onsen Town == The onsen town clusters around {{ill|Minakami Station|ja|水上駅|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minakami Station|qq=}} on the {{ill|Jōetsu Line|ja|上越線|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōetsu Line|qq=}}, with hotels and inns densely packed along the steep banks of the upper {{ill|Tone River|ja|利根川|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tone River|qq=}}. The area is dotted with scenic spots such as {{ill|Suwa Gorge|ja|諏訪峡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Gorge|qq=}} and Minakami Gorge, attracting numerous visitors during the autumn foliage season. The slow-moving {{ill|Horse-drawn carriage|ja|トテ馬車|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Horse-drawn carriage|qq=}} rides are a signature attraction of Minakami Onsen. Bridges over the Tone River, such as Yuhara Bridge and Minakami Bridge, are known for their nighttime illumination. Traditional amusements, including {{ill|Smart ball|ja|スマートボール|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Smart ball|qq=}} parlors, evoke nostalgia and draw modern visitors. Despite its focus on overnight stays, day-use facilities are limited but increasing. Many inns now accommodate day-use visitors. Public facilities include the town-run "Yuterume Tanigawa" and the "Fureai Koryukan," which offers foot baths. == History == According to legend, Minakami Onsen was founded during the {{ill|Eiroku era|ja|永禄年間|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eiroku era|qq=}} when the monk Kaiō discovered steam rising from a cliff along the Tone River. Before the opening of the {{ill|Shimizu Tunnel|ja|清水トンネル|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimizu Tunnel|qq=}}, the area was a remote mountain retreat. Writers like {{ill|Bokusui Wakayama|ja|若山牧水|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bokusui Wakayama|qq=}}, {{ill|Osamu Dazai|ja|太宰治|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Osamu Dazai|qq=}}, {{ill|Hakushū Kitahara|ja|北原白秋|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakushū Kitahara|qq=}}, and {{ill|Akiko Yosano|ja|与謝野晶子|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akiko Yosano|qq=}} were captivated by the harmony of hot springs and dramatic valleys. The area was then known as Yuhara Onsen, a name still in occasional use. The opening of the {{ill|Jōetsu Line|ja|上越線|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōetsu Line|qq=}} in 1931 brought Minakami Onsen closer to the {{ill|Greater Tokyo Area|ja|首都圏|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Greater Tokyo Area|qq=}}. The 1966 {{ill|Kikufuji Hotel fire|ja|菊富士ホテル火災|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kikufuji Hotel fire|qq=}} marked a significant event in its history. The construction of the {{ill|Kan-Etsu Expressway|ja|関越自動車道|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kan-Etsu Expressway|qq=}} and the introduction of express trains like the {{ill|Yukemuri Express|ja|水上列車|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yukemuri Express|qq=}} led to its rise as a major recreation destination. However, the shift towards group tours and nightlife transformed the onsen into a bustling entertainment hub, complete with bars and show pubs. Following the {{ill|economic bubble collapse|ja|バブル崩壊|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/economic bubble collapse|qq=}}, declining group tours and changing tourist preferences forced the onsen to refocus. Today, it aims to leverage its natural surroundings, integrating {{ill|skiing|ja|スキー|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/skiing|qq=}}, {{ill|rafting|ja|ラフティング|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/rafting|qq=}}, and other outdoor activities to develop as a recreational resort. == Recent Issues == The {{ill|Ōshimizu Tunnel|ja|大清水トンネル|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōshimizu Tunnel|qq=}} of the {{ill|Jōetsu Shinkansen|ja|上越新幹線|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōetsu Shinkansen|qq=}} passes 50 meters beneath the hot spring's aquifers, causing decreased water flow. In 1979, the issue was addressed in the {{ill|House of Representatives of Japan|ja|衆議院|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/House of Representatives of Japan|qq=}}, prompting the {{ill|Japan Railway Construction Public Corporation|ja|日本鉄道建設公団|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japan Railway Construction Public Corporation|qq=}} to drill new wells. The 2004 {{ill|onsen fraud scandal|ja|温泉偽装問題|1=Onsen Fraud Controversy|qq=Onsen Fraud Scandal|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/onsen fraud scandal|13=User:Immanuelle/Onsen Fraud Controversy|qq=}} revealed that some facilities used tap water while advertising natural hot springs, raising concerns over misrepresentation and improper tax collection. The {{ill|abandoned Hotel Ōmiya|ja|ホテル大宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/abandoned Hotel Ōmiya|qq=}}, located near Minakami Station, remains an unresolved issue for the local community. == Access == * Train: {{ill|Jōetsu Line|ja|上越線|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōetsu Line|qq=}}, Minakami Station, approximately 5 minutes on foot * Car: Exit at Minakami Interchange on the {{ill|Kan-Etsu Expressway|ja|関越自動車道|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kan-Etsu Expressway|qq=}}, via National Route 291 == References == {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * ''Disappearing Ruins of Japan: Hidden History Through Ruins,'' Taiyo Publishing, 2014 == See Also == * {{ill|Ii Yudana|ja|いい湯だな|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ii Yudana|qq=}} (song by {{ill|The Dukes Aces|ja|デューク・エイセス|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/The Dukes Aces|qq=}}) - Features Minakami in the fourth verse == External Links == * [https://www.enjoy-minakami.jp/ Minakami Perfect Guide] - Official tourism website of Minakami {{Onsen}} {{translated page|ja|水上温泉|version=95165560|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Onsen in Gunma Prefecture]] [[Category:Geography of Minakami]] [[Category:Minakami Onsen]] [[Category:Sulfate springs]] [[Category:Copper-Iron Spring]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] }} [[sn:Minakami Onsen]] [[ja:水上温泉]] [[en:Minakami Onsen]]

The 2004 {{ill|onsen fraud scandal|ja|温泉偽装問題|1=Onsen Fraud Controversy|qq=Onsen Fraud Scandal|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/onsen fraud scandal|13=User:Immanuelle/Onsen Fraud Controversy|qq=}} revealed that some facilities used tap water while advertising natural hot springs, raising concerns over misrepresentation and improper tax collection. The {{ill|abandoned Hotel Ōmiya|ja|ホテル大宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/abandoned Hotel Ōmiya|qq=}}, located near Minakami Station, remains an unresolved issue for the local community. == Access == * Train: {{ill|Jōetsu Line|ja|上越線|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōetsu Line|qq=}}, Minakami Station, approximately 5 minutes on foot * Car: Exit at Minakami Interchange on the {{ill|Kan-Etsu Expressway|ja|関越自動車道|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kan-Etsu Expressway|qq=}}, via National Route 291 == References == {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * ''Disappearing Ruins of Japan: Hidden History Through Ruins,'' Taiyo Publishing, 2014 == See Also == * {{ill|Ii Yudana|ja|いい湯だな|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ii Yudana|qq=}} (song by {{ill|The Dukes Aces|ja|デューク・エイセス|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/The Dukes Aces|qq=}}) - Features Minakami in the fourth verse == External Links == * [https://www.enjoy-minakami.jp/ Minakami Perfect Guide] - Official tourism website of Minakami {{Onsen}} {{translated page|ja|水上温泉|version=95165560|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Onsen in Gunma Prefecture]] [[Category:Geography of Minakami]] [[Category:Minakami Onsen]] [[Category:Sulfate springs]] [[Category:Copper-Iron Spring]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] }} [[sn:Minakami Onsen]] [[ja:水上温泉]] [[en:Minakami Onsen]]

revision.text:
{{Unreferenced|date=March 2017}} {{Onsen in Japan |Name = Minakami Onsen |image = Minakami Onsen 01.jpg |Caption = {{ill|Tone River|ja|利根川|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tone River|qq=}} riverside view at Minakami-kan |Location = {{ill|Minakami|ja|みなかみ町|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minakami|qq=}}, Tone District, Gunma Prefecture | Latitude degrees = 36 | Latitude minutes = 45 | Latitude seconds = 59 | Longitude degrees = 138 | Longitude minutes = 57 | Longitude seconds = 29 |Access = Train: {{ill|Jōetsu Line|ja|上越線|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōetsu Line|qq=}}, alight at Minakami Station, approximately 5 minutes on foot
Car: Exit at {{ill|Minakami Interchange|ja|水上インターチェンジ|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minakami Interchange|qq=}} on {{ill|Kan-Etsu Expressway|ja|関越自動車道|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kan-Etsu Expressway|qq=}}, follow National Route 291 |Hot spring type = {{ill|Sulfate spring|ja|硫酸塩泉|1=Sulfate spring|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sulfate spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Sulfate spring|qq=}} |Flow rate = |pH = |Classification of fluid = |Classification of osmotic pressure = |Number of accommodations = |Total capacity = |Annual bathers = |Year of statistics = |External link = http://www.minakamionsen.com/ |Remarks = }} '''Minakami Onsen''' (水上温泉) is an onsen located in {{ill|Minakami|ja|みなかみ町|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minakami|qq=}}, Tone District, Gunma Prefecture. The area south of {{ill|Mount Tanigawa|ja|谷川岳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Tanigawa|qq=}} is known as {{ill|Minakami Onsenkyo|ja|水上温泉郷|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minakami Onsenkyo|qq=}}, with Minakami Onsen serving as its central hub. Following {{ill|World War II|ja|第二次世界大戦|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/World War II|qq=}}, its excellent {{ill|transportation access|ja|交通アクセス|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/transportation access|qq=}} facilitated rapid expansion, establishing it as one of the major onsen areas in the prefecture alongside {{ill|Kusatsu Onsen|ja|草津温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kusatsu Onsen|qq=}} and {{ill|Ikaho Onsen|ja|伊香保温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikaho Onsen|qq=}}. In the {{ill|Jomo Karuta|ja|上毛かるた|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jomo Karuta|qq=}}, it is celebrated as "Mi no fuda: Minakami-Tanigawa, skiing and climbing." == Hot Spring Properties == * Calcium - {{ill|Sulfate spring|ja|硫酸塩泉|1=Sulfate spring|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sulfate spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Sulfate spring|qq=}} == Onsen Town == The onsen town clusters around {{ill|Minakami Station|ja|水上駅|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minakami Station|qq=}} on the {{ill|Jōetsu Line|ja|上越線|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōetsu Line|qq=}}, with hotels and inns densely packed along the steep banks of the upper {{ill|Tone River|ja|利根川|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tone River|qq=}}. The area is dotted with scenic spots such as {{ill|Suwa Gorge|ja|諏訪峡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Gorge|qq=}} and Minakami Gorge, attracting numerous visitors during the autumn foliage season. The slow-moving {{ill|Horse-drawn carriage|ja|トテ馬車|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Horse-drawn carriage|qq=}} rides are a signature attraction of Minakami Onsen. Bridges over the Tone River, such as Yuhara Bridge and Minakami Bridge, are known for their nighttime illumination. Traditional amusements, including {{ill|Smart ball|ja|スマートボール|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Smart ball|qq=}} parlors, evoke nostalgia and draw modern visitors. Despite its focus on overnight stays, day-use facilities are limited but increasing. Many inns now accommodate day-use visitors. Public facilities include the town-run "Yuterume Tanigawa" and the "Fureai Koryukan," which offers foot baths. == History == According to legend, Minakami Onsen was founded during the {{ill|Eiroku era|ja|永禄年間|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eiroku era|qq=}} when the monk Kaiō discovered steam rising from a cliff along the Tone River. Before the opening of the {{ill|Shimizu Tunnel|ja|清水トンネル|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimizu Tunnel|qq=}}, the area was a remote mountain retreat. Writers like {{ill|Bokusui Wakayama|ja|若山牧水|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bokusui Wakayama|qq=}}, {{ill|Osamu Dazai|ja|太宰治|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Osamu Dazai|qq=}}, {{ill|Hakushū Kitahara|ja|北原白秋|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakushū Kitahara|qq=}}, and {{ill|Akiko Yosano|ja|与謝野晶子|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akiko Yosano|qq=}} were captivated by the harmony of hot springs and dramatic valleys. The area was then known as Yuhara Onsen, a name still in occasional use. The opening of the {{ill|Jōetsu Line|ja|上越線|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōetsu Line|qq=}} in 1931 brought Minakami Onsen closer to the {{ill|Greater Tokyo Area|ja|首都圏|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Greater Tokyo Area|qq=}}. The 1966 {{ill|Kikufuji Hotel fire|ja|菊富士ホテル火災|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kikufuji Hotel fire|qq=}} marked a significant event in its history. The construction of the {{ill|Kan-Etsu Expressway|ja|関越自動車道|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kan-Etsu Expressway|qq=}} and the introduction of express trains like the {{ill|Yukemuri Express|ja|水上列車|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yukemuri Express|qq=}} led to its rise as a major recreation destination. However, the shift towards group tours and nightlife transformed the onsen into a bustling entertainment hub, complete with bars and show pubs. Following the {{ill|economic bubble collapse|ja|バブル崩壊|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/economic bubble collapse|qq=}}, declining group tours and changing tourist preferences forced the onsen to refocus. Today, it aims to leverage its natural surroundings, integrating {{ill|skiing|ja|スキー|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/skiing|qq=}}, {{ill|rafting|ja|ラフティング|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/rafting|qq=}}, and other outdoor activities to develop as a recreational resort. == Recent Issues == The {{ill|Ōshimizu Tunnel|ja|大清水トンネル|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōshimizu Tunnel|qq=}} of the {{ill|Jōetsu Shinkansen|ja|上越新幹線|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōetsu Shinkansen|qq=}} passes 50 meters beneath the hot spring's aquifers, causing decreased water flow. In 1979, the issue was addressed in the {{ill|House of Representatives of Japan|ja|衆議院|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/House of Representatives of Japan|qq=}}, prompting the {{ill|Japan Railway Construction Public Corporation|ja|日本鉄道建設公団|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japan Railway Construction Public Corporation|qq=}} to drill new wells. The 2004 {{ill|onsen fraud scandal|ja|温泉偽装問題|1=Onsen Fraud Controversy|qq=Onsen Fraud Scandal|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/onsen fraud scandal|13=User:Immanuelle/Onsen Fraud Controversy|qq=}} revealed that some facilities used tap water while advertising natural hot springs, raising concerns over misrepresentation and improper tax collection. The {{ill|abandoned Hotel Ōmiya|ja|ホテル大宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/abandoned Hotel Ōmiya|qq=}}, located near Minakami Station, remains an unresolved issue for the local community. == Access == * Train: {{ill|Jōetsu Line|ja|上越線|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōetsu Line|qq=}}, Minakami Station, approximately 5 minutes on foot * Car: Exit at Minakami Interchange on the {{ill|Kan-Etsu Expressway|ja|関越自動車道|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kan-Etsu Expressway|qq=}}, via National Route 291 == References == {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * ''Disappearing Ruins of Japan: Hidden History Through Ruins,'' Taiyo Publishing, 2014 == See Also == * {{ill|Ii Yudana|ja|いい湯だな|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ii Yudana|qq=}} (song by {{ill|The Dukes Aces|ja|デューク・エイセス|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/The Dukes Aces|qq=}}) - Features Minakami in the fourth verse == External Links == * [https://www.enjoy-minakami.jp/ Minakami Perfect Guide] - Official tourism website of Minakami {{Onsen}} {{translated page|ja|水上温泉|version=95165560|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Onsen in Gunma Prefecture]] [[Category:Geography of Minakami]] [[Category:Sulfate springs]] [[Category:Copper-Iron Spring]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] }} [[sn:Minakami Onsen]] [[ja:水上温泉]] [[en:Minakami Onsen]]

Today, it aims to leverage its natural surroundings, integrating [[:en:Skiing|skiing]], [[:en:Rafting|rafting]], and other outdoor activities to develop as a recreational resort. == Recent Issues == The {{ill|Ōshimizu Tunnel|ja|大清水トンネル|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōshimizu Tunnel|qq=}} of the [[:en:Jōetsu Shinkansen|Jōetsu Shinkansen]] passes 50 meters beneath the hot spring's aquifers, causing decreased water flow. In 1979, the issue was addressed in the [[:en:House of Representatives (Japan)|House of Representatives of Japan]], prompting the [[:en:Japan Railway Construction Public Corporation|Japan Railway Construction Public Corporation]] to drill new wells. The 2004 {{ill|onsen fraud scandal|ja|温泉偽装問題|1=Onsen Fraud Controversy|qq=Onsen Fraud Scandal|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/onsen fraud scandal|13=User:Immanuelle/Onsen Fraud Controversy|qq=}} revealed that some facilities used tap water while advertising natural hot springs, raising concerns over misrepresentation and improper tax collection. The {{ill|abandoned Hotel Ōmiya|ja|ホテル大宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/abandoned Hotel Ōmiya|qq=}}, located near Minakami Station, remains an unresolved issue for the local community. == Access == * Train: [[:en:Jōetsu Line|Jōetsu Line]], Minakami Station, approximately 5 minutes on foot * Car: Exit at Minakami Interchange on the [[:en:Kan-etsu Expressway|Kan-Etsu Expressway]], via National Route 291 == References == {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * ''Disappearing Ruins of Japan: Hidden History Through Ruins,'' Taiyo Publishing, 2014 == See Also == * {{ill|Ii Yudana|ja|いい湯だな|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ii Yudana|qq=}} (song by {{ill|The Dukes Aces|ja|デューク・エイセス|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/The Dukes Aces|qq=}}) - Features Minakami in the fourth verse == External Links == * [https://www.enjoy-minakami.jp/ Minakami Perfect Guide] - Official tourism website of Minakami {{Onsen}} {{translated page|ja|水上温泉|version=95165560|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Onsen in Gunma Prefecture]] [[Category:Geography of Minakami]] [[Category:Sulfate springs]] [[Category:Copper-Iron Spring]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] }} [[sn:Minakami Onsen]] [[ja:水上温泉]] [[en:Minakami Onsen]] [[Category:みなかみ町の地理]]

revision.comment: Bot: add category Category:Hot springs of Gunma Prefecture
revision.text:
{{Unreferenced|date=March 2017}} {{Onsen in Japan |Name = Minakami Onsen |image = Minakami Onsen 01.jpg |Caption = [[:en:Tone River|Tone River]] riverside view at Minakami-kan |Location = [[:en:Minakami, Gunma|Minakami]], Tone District, Gunma Prefecture | Latitude degrees = 36 | Latitude minutes = 45 | Latitude seconds = 59 | Longitude degrees = 138 | Longitude minutes = 57 | Longitude seconds = 29 |Access = Train: [[:en:Jōetsu Line|Jōetsu Line]], alight at Minakami Station, approximately 5 minutes on foot
Car: Exit at {{ill|Minakami Interchange|ja|水上インターチェンジ|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minakami Interchange|qq=}} on [[:en:Kan-etsu Expressway|Kan-Etsu Expressway]], follow National Route 291 |Hot spring type = {{ill|Sulfate spring|ja|硫酸塩泉|1=Sulfate spring|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sulfate spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Sulfate spring|qq=}} |Flow rate = |pH = |Classification of fluid = |Classification of osmotic pressure = |Number of accommodations = |Total capacity = |Annual bathers = |Year of statistics = |External link = http://www.minakamionsen.com/ |Remarks = }} '''Minakami Onsen''' (水上温泉) is an onsen located in [[:en:Minakami, Gunma|Minakami]], Tone District, Gunma Prefecture. The area south of [[:en:Mount Tanigawa|Mount Tanigawa]] is known as {{ill|Minakami Onsenkyo|ja|水上温泉郷|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minakami Onsenkyo|qq=}}, with Minakami Onsen serving as its central hub. Following [[:en:World War II|World War II]], its excellent {{ill|transportation access|ja|交通アクセス|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/transportation access|qq=}} facilitated rapid expansion, establishing it as one of the major onsen areas in the prefecture alongside [[:en:Kusatsu Onsen|Kusatsu Onsen]] and {{ill|Ikaho Onsen|ja|伊香保温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikaho Onsen|qq=}}. In the {{ill|Jomo Karuta|ja|上毛かるた|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jomo Karuta|qq=}}, it is celebrated as "Mi no fuda: Minakami-Tanigawa, skiing and climbing." == Hot Spring Properties == * Calcium - {{ill|Sulfate spring|ja|硫酸塩泉|1=Sulfate spring|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sulfate spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Sulfate spring|qq=}} == Onsen Town == The onsen town clusters around [[:en:Minakami Station|Minakami Station]] on the [[:en:Jōetsu Line|Jōetsu Line]], with hotels and inns densely packed along the steep banks of the upper [[:en:Tone River|Tone River]]. The area is dotted with scenic spots such as {{ill|Suwa Gorge|ja|諏訪峡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Gorge|qq=}} and Minakami Gorge, attracting numerous visitors during the autumn foliage season. The slow-moving {{ill|Horse-drawn carriage|ja|トテ馬車|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Horse-drawn carriage|qq=}} rides are a signature attraction of Minakami Onsen. Bridges over the Tone River, such as Yuhara Bridge and Minakami Bridge, are known for their nighttime illumination. Traditional amusements, including {{ill|Smart ball|ja|スマートボール|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Smart ball|qq=}} parlors, evoke nostalgia and draw modern visitors. Despite its focus on overnight stays, day-use facilities are limited but increasing. Many inns now accommodate day-use visitors. Public facilities include the town-run "Yuterume Tanigawa" and the "Fureai Koryukan," which offers foot baths. == History == According to legend, Minakami Onsen was founded during the {{ill|Eiroku era|ja|永禄年間|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eiroku era|qq=}} when the monk Kaiō discovered steam rising from a cliff along the Tone River. Before the opening of the {{ill|Shimizu Tunnel|ja|清水トンネル|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimizu Tunnel|qq=}}, the area was a remote mountain retreat. Writers like [[:en:Bokusui Wakayama|Bokusui Wakayama]], [[:en:Osamu Dazai|Osamu Dazai]], [[:en:Hakushū Kitahara|Hakushū Kitahara]], and [[:en:Yosano Akiko|Akiko Yosano]] were captivated by the harmony of hot springs and dramatic valleys. The area was then known as Yuhara Onsen, a name still in occasional use. The opening of the [[:en:Jōetsu Line|Jōetsu Line]] in 1931 brought Minakami Onsen closer to the [[:en:Capital region|Greater Tokyo Area]]. The 1966 {{ill|Kikufuji Hotel fire|ja|菊富士ホテル火災|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kikufuji Hotel fire|qq=}} marked a significant event in its history. The construction of the [[:en:Kan-etsu Expressway|Kan-Etsu Expressway]] and the introduction of express trains like the {{ill|Yukemuri Express|ja|水上列車|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yukemuri Express|qq=}} led to its rise as a major recreation destination. However, the shift towards group tours and nightlife transformed the onsen into a bustling entertainment hub, complete with bars and show pubs. Following the [[:en:Lost Decades|economic bubble collapse]], declining group tours and changing tourist preferences forced the onsen to refocus. Today, it aims to leverage its natural surroundings, integrating [[:en:Skiing|skiing]], [[:en:Rafting|rafting]], and other outdoor activities to develop as a recreational resort. == Recent Issues == The {{ill|Ōshimizu Tunnel|ja|大清水トンネル|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōshimizu Tunnel|qq=}} of the [[:en:Jōetsu Shinkansen|Jōetsu Shinkansen]] passes 50 meters beneath the hot spring's aquifers, causing decreased water flow. In 1979, the issue was addressed in the [[:en:House of Representatives (Japan)|House of Representatives of Japan]], prompting the [[:en:Japan Railway Construction Public Corporation|Japan Railway Construction Public Corporation]] to drill new wells. The 2004 {{ill|onsen fraud scandal|ja|温泉偽装問題|1=Onsen Fraud Controversy|qq=Onsen Fraud Scandal|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/onsen fraud scandal|13=User:Immanuelle/Onsen Fraud Controversy|qq=}} revealed that some facilities used tap water while advertising natural hot springs, raising concerns over misrepresentation and improper tax collection. The {{ill|abandoned Hotel Ōmiya|ja|ホテル大宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/abandoned Hotel Ōmiya|qq=}}, located near Minakami Station, remains an unresolved issue for the local community. == Access == * Train: [[:en:Jōetsu Line|Jōetsu Line]], Minakami Station, approximately 5 minutes on foot * Car: Exit at Minakami Interchange on the [[:en:Kan-etsu Expressway|Kan-Etsu Expressway]], via National Route 291 == References == {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * ''Disappearing Ruins of Japan: Hidden History Through Ruins,'' Taiyo Publishing, 2014 == See Also == * {{ill|Ii Yudana|ja|いい湯だな|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ii Yudana|qq=}} (song by {{ill|The Dukes Aces|ja|デューク・エイセス|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/The Dukes Aces|qq=}}) - Features Minakami in the fourth verse == External Links == * [https://www.enjoy-minakami.jp/ Minakami Perfect Guide] - Official tourism website of Minakami {{Onsen}} {{translated page|ja|水上温泉|version=95165560|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Onsen in Gunma Prefecture]] [[Category:Geography of Minakami]] [[Category:Sulfate springs]] [[Category:Copper-Iron Spring]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] }} [[sn:Minakami Onsen]] [[ja:水上温泉]] [[en:Minakami Onsen]] [[Category:みなかみ町の地理]] [[Category:Hot springs of Gunma Prefecture]]

{{Unreferenced|date=March 2017}} {{Onsen in Japan |Name = Minakami Onsen |image = Minakami Onsen 01.jpg |Caption = [[:en:Tone River|Tone River]] riverside view at Minakami-kan |Location = [[:en:Minakami, Gunma|Minakami]], Tone District, Gunma Prefecture | Latitude degrees = 36 | Latitude minutes = 45 | Latitude seconds = 59 | Longitude degrees = 138 | Longitude minutes = 57 | Longitude seconds = 29 |Access = Train: [[:en:Jōetsu Line|Jōetsu Line]], alight at Minakami Station, approximately 5 minutes on foot
Car: Exit at {{ill|Minakami Interchange|ja|水上インターチェンジ|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minakami Interchange|qq=}} on [[:en:Kan-etsu Expressway|Kan-Etsu Expressway]], follow National Route 291 |Hot spring type = {{ill|Sulfate spring|ja|硫酸塩泉|1=Sulfate spring|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sulfate spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Sulfate spring|qq=}} |Flow rate = |pH = |Classification of fluid = |Classification of osmotic pressure = |Number of accommodations = |Total capacity = |Annual bathers = |Year of statistics = |External link = http://www.minakamionsen.com/ |Remarks = }} '''Minakami Onsen''' (水上温泉) is an onsen located in [[:en:Minakami, Gunma|Minakami]], Tone District, Gunma Prefecture. The area south of [[:en:Mount Tanigawa|Mount Tanigawa]] is known as {{ill|Minakami Onsenkyo|ja|水上温泉郷|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minakami Onsenkyo|qq=}}, with Minakami Onsen serving as its central hub. Following [[:en:World War II|World War II]], its excellent {{ill|transportation access|ja|交通アクセス|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/transportation access|qq=}} facilitated rapid expansion, establishing it as one of the major onsen areas in the prefecture alongside [[:en:Kusatsu Onsen|Kusatsu Onsen]] and {{ill|Ikaho Onsen|ja|伊香保温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikaho Onsen|qq=}}. In the {{ill|Jomo Karuta|ja|上毛かるた|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jomo Karuta|qq=}}, it is celebrated as "Mi no fuda: Minakami-Tanigawa, skiing and climbing." == Hot Spring Properties == * Calcium - {{ill|Sulfate spring|ja|硫酸塩泉|1=Sulfate spring|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sulfate spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Sulfate spring|qq=}} == Onsen Town == The onsen town clusters around [[:en:Minakami Station|Minakami Station]] on the [[:en:Jōetsu Line|Jōetsu Line]], with hotels and inns densely packed along the steep banks of the upper [[:en:Tone River|Tone River]]. The area is dotted with scenic spots such as {{ill|Suwa Gorge|ja|諏訪峡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Gorge|qq=}} and Minakami Gorge, attracting numerous visitors during the autumn foliage season. The slow-moving {{ill|Horse-drawn carriage|ja|トテ馬車|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Horse-drawn carriage|qq=}} rides are a signature attraction of Minakami Onsen. Bridges over the Tone River, such as Yuhara Bridge and Minakami Bridge, are known for their nighttime illumination. Traditional amusements, including {{ill|Smart ball|ja|スマートボール|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Smart ball|qq=}} parlors, evoke nostalgia and draw modern visitors. Despite its focus on overnight stays, day-use facilities are limited but increasing. Many inns now accommodate day-use visitors. Public facilities include the town-run "Yuterume Tanigawa" and the "Fureai Koryukan," which offers foot baths. == History == According to legend, Minakami Onsen was founded during the {{ill|Eiroku era|ja|永禄年間|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eiroku era|qq=}} when the monk Kaiō discovered steam rising from a cliff along the Tone River. Before the opening of the {{ill|Shimizu Tunnel|ja|清水トンネル|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimizu Tunnel|qq=}}, the area was a remote mountain retreat. Writers like [[:en:Bokusui Wakayama|Bokusui Wakayama]], [[:en:Osamu Dazai|Osamu Dazai]], [[:en:Hakushū Kitahara|Hakushū Kitahara]], and [[:en:Yosano Akiko|Akiko Yosano]] were captivated by the harmony of hot springs and dramatic valleys. The area was then known as Yuhara Onsen, a name still in occasional use. The opening of the [[:en:Jōetsu Line|Jōetsu Line]] in 1931 brought Minakami Onsen closer to the [[:en:Capital region|Greater Tokyo Area]]. The 1966 {{ill|Kikufuji Hotel fire|ja|菊富士ホテル火災|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kikufuji Hotel fire|qq=}} marked a significant event in its history. The construction of the [[:en:Kan-etsu Expressway|Kan-Etsu Expressway]] and the introduction of express trains like the {{ill|Yukemuri Express|ja|水上列車|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yukemuri Express|qq=}} led to its rise as a major recreation destination. However, the shift towards group tours and nightlife transformed the onsen into a bustling entertainment hub, complete with bars and show pubs. Following the [[:en:Lost Decades|economic bubble collapse]], declining group tours and changing tourist preferences forced the onsen to refocus. Today, it aims to leverage its natural surroundings, integrating [[:en:Skiing|skiing]], [[:en:Rafting|rafting]], and other outdoor activities to develop as a recreational resort. == Recent Issues == The {{ill|Ōshimizu Tunnel|ja|大清水トンネル|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōshimizu Tunnel|qq=}} of the [[:en:Jōetsu Shinkansen|Jōetsu Shinkansen]] passes 50 meters beneath the hot spring's aquifers, causing decreased water flow. In 1979, the issue was addressed in the [[:en:House of Representatives (Japan)|House of Representatives of Japan]], prompting the [[:en:Japan Railway Construction Public Corporation|Japan Railway Construction Public Corporation]] to drill new wells. The 2004 {{ill|onsen fraud scandal|ja|温泉偽装問題|1=Onsen Fraud Controversy|qq=Onsen Fraud Scandal|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/onsen fraud scandal|13=User:Immanuelle/Onsen Fraud Controversy|qq=}} revealed that some facilities used tap water while advertising natural hot springs, raising concerns over misrepresentation and improper tax collection. The {{ill|abandoned Hotel Ōmiya|ja|ホテル大宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/abandoned Hotel Ōmiya|qq=}}, located near Minakami Station, remains an unresolved issue for the local community. == Access == * Train: [[:en:Jōetsu Line|Jōetsu Line]], Minakami Station, approximately 5 minutes on foot * Car: Exit at Minakami Interchange on the [[:en:Kan-etsu Expressway|Kan-Etsu Expressway]], via National Route 291 == References == {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * ''Disappearing Ruins of Japan: Hidden History Through Ruins,'' Taiyo Publishing, 2014 == See Also == * {{ill|Ii Yudana|ja|いい湯だな|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ii Yudana|qq=}} (song by {{ill|The Dukes Aces|ja|デューク・エイセス|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/The Dukes Aces|qq=}}) - Features Minakami in the fourth verse == External Links == * [https://www.enjoy-minakami.jp/ Minakami Perfect Guide] - Official tourism website of Minakami {{Onsen}} {{translated page|ja|水上温泉|version=95165560|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Onsen in Gunma Prefecture]] [[Category:Geography of Minakami]] [[Category:Sulfate springs]] [[Category:Copper-Iron Spring]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] }} [[sn:Minakami Onsen]] [[ja:水上温泉]] [[Category:Geography of Minakami, Gunma]] [[Category:Hot springs of Gunma Prefecture]] {{Commons category|Minakami Onsen}}

The area south of [[:en:Mount Tanigawa|Mount Tanigawa]] is known as {{ill|Minakami Onsenkyo|ja|水上温泉郷|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minakami Onsenkyo|qq=}}, with Minakami Onsen serving as its central hub. Following [[:en:World War II|World War II]], its excellent {{ill|transportation access|ja|交通アクセス|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/transportation access|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to アクセス}} facilitated rapid expansion, establishing it as one of the major onsen areas in the prefecture alongside [[:en:Kusatsu Onsen|Kusatsu Onsen]] and {{ill|Ikaho Onsen|ja|伊香保温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikaho Onsen|qq=}}. In the {{ill|Jomo Karuta|ja|上毛かるた|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jomo Karuta|qq=}}, it is celebrated as "Mi no fuda: Minakami-Tanigawa, skiing and climbing." == Hot Spring Properties == * Calcium - {{ill|Sulfate spring|ja|硫酸塩泉|1=Sulfate spring|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sulfate spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Sulfate spring|qq=}} == Onsen Town == The onsen town clusters around [[:en:Minakami Station|Minakami Station]] on the [[:en:Jōetsu Line|Jōetsu Line]], with hotels and inns densely packed along the steep banks of the upper [[:en:Tone River|Tone River]]. The area is dotted with scenic spots such as {{ill|Suwa Gorge|ja|諏訪峡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Gorge|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and Minakami Gorge, attracting numerous visitors during the autumn foliage season. The slow-moving {{ill|Horse-drawn carriage|ja|トテ馬車|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Horse-drawn carriage|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 馬車}} rides are a signature attraction of Minakami Onsen. Bridges over the Tone River, such as Yuhara Bridge and Minakami Bridge, are known for their nighttime illumination. Traditional amusements, including {{ill|Smart ball|ja|スマートボール|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Smart ball|qq=}} parlors, evoke nostalgia and draw modern visitors. Despite its focus on overnight stays, day-use facilities are limited but increasing. Many inns now accommodate day-use visitors. Public facilities include the town-run "Yuterume Tanigawa" and the "Fureai Koryukan," which offers foot baths. == History == According to legend, Minakami Onsen was founded during the {{ill|Eiroku era|ja|永禄年間|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eiroku era|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} when the monk Kaiō discovered steam rising from a cliff along the Tone River. Before the opening of the {{ill|Shimizu Tunnel|ja|清水トンネル|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimizu Tunnel|qq=}}, the area was a remote mountain retreat. Writers like [[:en:Bokusui Wakayama|Bokusui Wakayama]], [[:en:Osamu Dazai|Osamu Dazai]], [[:en:Hakushū Kitahara|Hakushū Kitahara]], and [[:en:Yosano Akiko|Akiko Yosano]] were captivated by the harmony of hot springs and dramatic valleys. The area was then known as Yuhara Onsen, a name still in occasional use. The opening of the [[:en:Jōetsu Line|Jōetsu Line]] in 1931 brought Minakami Onsen closer to the [[:en:Capital region|Greater Tokyo Area]]. The 1966 {{ill|Kikufuji Hotel fire|ja|菊富士ホテル火災|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kikufuji Hotel fire|qq=}} marked a significant event in its history. The construction of the [[:en:Kan-etsu Expressway|Kan-Etsu Expressway]] and the introduction of express trains like the {{ill|Yukemuri Express|ja|水上列車|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yukemuri Express|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} led to its rise as a major recreation destination. However, the shift towards group tours and nightlife transformed the onsen into a bustling entertainment hub, complete with bars and show pubs. Following the [[:en:Lost Decades|economic bubble collapse]], declining group tours and changing tourist preferences forced the onsen to refocus. Today, it aims to leverage its natural surroundings, integrating [[:en:Skiing|skiing]], [[:en:Rafting|rafting]], and other outdoor activities to develop as a recreational resort. == Recent Issues == The {{ill|Ōshimizu Tunnel|ja|大清水トンネル|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōshimizu Tunnel|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 清水トンネル}} of the [[:en:Jōetsu Shinkansen|Jōetsu Shinkansen]] passes 50 meters beneath the hot spring's aquifers, causing decreased water flow. In 1979, the issue was addressed in the [[:en:House of Representatives (Japan)|House of Representatives of Japan]], prompting the [[:en:Japan Railway Construction Public Corporation|Japan Railway Construction Public Corporation]] to drill new wells. The 2004 {{ill|onsen fraud scandal|ja|温泉偽装問題|1=Onsen Fraud Controversy|qq=Onsen Fraud Scandal|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/onsen fraud scandal|13=User:Immanuelle/Onsen Fraud Controversy|qq=}} revealed that some facilities used tap water while advertising natural hot springs, raising concerns over misrepresentation and improper tax collection. The {{ill|abandoned Hotel Ōmiya|ja|ホテル大宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/abandoned Hotel Ōmiya|qq=}}, located near Minakami Station, remains an unresolved issue for the local community. == Access == * Train: [[:en:Jōetsu Line|Jōetsu Line]], Minakami Station, approximately 5 minutes on foot * Car: Exit at Minakami Interchange on the [[:en:Kan-etsu Expressway|Kan-Etsu Expressway]], via National Route 291 == References == {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * ''Disappearing Ruins of Japan: Hidden History Through Ruins,'' Taiyo Publishing, 2014 == See Also == * {{ill|Ii Yudana|ja|いい湯だな|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ii Yudana|qq=}} (song by {{ill|The Dukes Aces|ja|デューク・エイセス|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/The Dukes Aces|qq=}}) - Features Minakami in the fourth verse == External Links == * [https://www.enjoy-minakami.jp/ Minakami Perfect Guide] - Official tourism website of Minakami {{Onsen}} {{translated page|ja|水上温泉|version=95165560|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Sulfate springs]] [[Category:Copper-Iron Spring]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] }} [[sn:Minakami Onsen]] [[ja:水上温泉]] [[Category:Hot springs of Gunma Prefecture]] {{Commons category|Minakami Onsen}}

In the {{ill|Jomo Karuta|ja|上毛かるた|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jomo Karuta|qq=|1=Jōmō Karuta|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17216027}}, it is celebrated as "Mi no fuda: Minakami-Tanigawa, skiing and climbing." == Hot Spring Properties == * Calcium - {{ill|Sulfate spring|ja|硫酸塩泉|1=Sulfate spring|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sulfate spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Sulfate spring|qq=}} == Onsen Town == The onsen town clusters around [[:en:Minakami Station|Minakami Station]] on the [[:en:Jōetsu Line|Jōetsu Line]], with hotels and inns densely packed along the steep banks of the upper [[:en:Tone River|Tone River]]. The area is dotted with scenic spots such as {{ill|Suwa Gorge|ja|諏訪峡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Gorge|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and Minakami Gorge, attracting numerous visitors during the autumn foliage season. The slow-moving {{ill|Horse-drawn carriage|ja|トテ馬車|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Horse-drawn carriage|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 馬車|1=horse-drawn vehicle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1112950}} rides are a signature attraction of Minakami Onsen. Bridges over the Tone River, such as Yuhara Bridge and Minakami Bridge, are known for their nighttime illumination. Traditional amusements, including {{ill|Smart ball|ja|スマートボール|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Smart ball|qq=|1=Smart ball|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11313289}} parlors, evoke nostalgia and draw modern visitors. Despite its focus on overnight stays, day-use facilities are limited but increasing. Many inns now accommodate day-use visitors. Public facilities include the town-run "Yuterume Tanigawa" and the "Fureai Koryukan," which offers foot baths. == History == According to legend, Minakami Onsen was founded during the {{ill|Eiroku era|ja|永禄年間|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eiroku era|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} when the monk Kaiō discovered steam rising from a cliff along the Tone River. Before the opening of the {{ill|Shimizu Tunnel|ja|清水トンネル|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimizu Tunnel|qq=|1=Shimizu Tunnels|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q75220055}}, the area was a remote mountain retreat. Writers like [[:en:Bokusui Wakayama|Bokusui Wakayama]], [[:en:Osamu Dazai|Osamu Dazai]], [[:en:Hakushū Kitahara|Hakushū Kitahara]], and [[:en:Yosano Akiko|Akiko Yosano]] were captivated by the harmony of hot springs and dramatic valleys. The area was then known as Yuhara Onsen, a name still in occasional use. The opening of the [[:en:Jōetsu Line|Jōetsu Line]] in 1931 brought Minakami Onsen closer to the [[:en:Capital region|Greater Tokyo Area]]. The 1966 {{ill|Kikufuji Hotel fire|ja|菊富士ホテル火災|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kikufuji Hotel fire|qq=|1=Kikufuji Hotel fire|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11619620}} marked a significant event in its history. The construction of the [[:en:Kan-etsu Expressway|Kan-Etsu Expressway]] and the introduction of express trains like the {{ill|Yukemuri Express|ja|水上列車|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yukemuri Express|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} led to its rise as a major recreation destination. However, the shift towards group tours and nightlife transformed the onsen into a bustling entertainment hub, complete with bars and show pubs. Following the [[:en:Lost Decades|economic bubble collapse]], declining group tours and changing tourist preferences forced the onsen to refocus. Today, it aims to leverage its natural surroundings, integrating [[:en:Skiing|skiing]], [[:en:Rafting|rafting]], and other outdoor activities to develop as a recreational resort. == Recent Issues == The {{ill|Ōshimizu Tunnel|ja|大清水トンネル|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōshimizu Tunnel|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 清水トンネル|1=Shimizu Tunnels|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q75220055}} of the [[:en:Jōetsu Shinkansen|Jōetsu Shinkansen]] passes 50 meters beneath the hot spring's aquifers, causing decreased water flow. In 1979, the issue was addressed in the [[:en:House of Representatives (Japan)|House of Representatives of Japan]], prompting the [[:en:Japan Railway Construction Public Corporation|Japan Railway Construction Public Corporation]] to drill new wells. The 2004 {{ill|onsen fraud scandal|ja|温泉偽装問題|1=Onsen Fraud Controversy|qq=Onsen Fraud Scandal|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/onsen fraud scandal|13=User:Immanuelle/Onsen Fraud Controversy|qq=}} revealed that some facilities used tap water while advertising natural hot springs, raising concerns over misrepresentation and improper tax collection. The {{ill|abandoned Hotel Ōmiya|ja|ホテル大宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/abandoned Hotel Ōmiya|qq=|1=Abandoned Hotel Ōmiya|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q85873736}}, located near Minakami Station, remains an unresolved issue for the local community. == Access == * Train: [[:en:Jōetsu Line|Jōetsu Line]], Minakami Station, approximately 5 minutes on foot * Car: Exit at Minakami Interchange on the [[:en:Kan-etsu Expressway|Kan-Etsu Expressway]], via National Route 291 == References == {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * ''Disappearing Ruins of Japan: Hidden History Through Ruins,'' Taiyo Publishing, 2014 == See Also == * {{ill|Ii Yudana|ja|いい湯だな|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ii Yudana|qq=|1=Ii Yudana|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11259008}} (song by {{ill|The Dukes Aces|ja|デューク・エイセス|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/The Dukes Aces|qq=|1=Duke Aces|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11320788}}) - Features Minakami in the fourth verse == External Links == * [https://www.enjoy-minakami.jp/ Minakami Perfect Guide] - Official tourism website of Minakami {{Onsen}} {{translated page|ja|水上温泉|version=95165560|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Sulfate spring]] [[Category:Copper-Iron Spring]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] }} [[sn:Minakami Onsen]] [[ja:水上温泉]] [[Category:Hot springs of Gunma Prefecture]] {{Commons category|Minakami Onsen}}

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11548228]] Wikidata name: Minakami Onsen [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:11, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Unreferenced|date=March 2017}} {{Onsen in Japan |Name = Minakami Onsen |image = Minakami Onsen 01.jpg |Caption = [[:en:Tone River|Tone River]] riverside view at Minakami-kan |Location = [[:en:Minakami, Gunma|Minakami]], Tone District, Gunma Prefecture | Latitude degrees = 36 | Latitude minutes = 45 | Latitude seconds = 59 | Longitude degrees = 138 | Longitude minutes = 57 | Longitude seconds = 29 |Access = Train: [[:en:Jōetsu Line|Jōetsu Line]], alight at Minakami Station, approximately 5 minutes on foot
Car: Exit at {{ill|Minakami Interchange|ja|水上インターチェンジ|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minakami Interchange|qq=|1=Minakami interchange|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11129404}} on [[:en:Kan-etsu Expressway|Kan-Etsu Expressway]], follow National Route 291 |Hot spring type = {{ill|Sulfate spring|ja|硫酸塩泉|1=Sulfate spring|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sulfate spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Sulfate spring|qq=}} |Flow rate = |pH = |Classification of fluid = |Classification of osmotic pressure = |Number of accommodations = |Total capacity = |Annual bathers = |Year of statistics = |External link = http://www.minakamionsen.com/ |Remarks = }} '''Minakami Onsen''' (水上温泉) is an onsen located in [[:en:Minakami, Gunma|Minakami]], Tone District, Gunma Prefecture. The area south of [[:en:Mount Tanigawa|Mount Tanigawa]] is known as {{ill|Minakami Onsenkyo|ja|水上温泉郷|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minakami Onsenkyo|qq=|1=Minakami Onsenkyō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11548229}}, with Minakami Onsen serving as its central hub. Following [[:en:World War II|World War II]], its excellent {{ill|transportation access|ja|交通アクセス|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/transportation access|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to アクセス|1=Access|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q338902}} facilitated rapid expansion, establishing it as one of the major onsen areas in the prefecture alongside [[:en:Kusatsu Onsen|Kusatsu Onsen]] and {{ill|Ikaho Onsen|ja|伊香保温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikaho Onsen|qq=}}. [[commons:Category:Minakami Onsen]][[ja:水上温泉]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11548228]] Wikidata name: Minakami Onsen [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:11, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Unreferenced|date=March 2017}} {{Onsen in Japan |Name = Minakami Onsen |image = Minakami Onsen 01.jpg |Caption = [[:en:Tone River|Tone River]] riverside view at Minakami-kan |Location = [[:en:Minakami, Gunma|Minakami]], Tone District, Gunma Prefecture | Latitude degrees = 36 | Latitude minutes = 45 | Latitude seconds = 59 | Longitude degrees = 138 | Longitude minutes = 57 | Longitude seconds = 29 |Access = Train: [[:en:Jōetsu Line|Jōetsu Line]], alight at Minakami Station, approximately 5 minutes on foot
Car: Exit at {{ill|Minakami Interchange|ja|水上インターチェンジ|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minakami Interchange|qq=|1=Minakami interchange|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11129404}} on [[:en:Kan-etsu Expressway|Kan-Etsu Expressway]], follow National Route 291 |Hot spring type = {{ill|Sulfate spring|ja|硫酸塩泉|1=Sulfate spring|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sulfate spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Sulfate spring|qq=}} |Flow rate = |pH = |Classification of fluid = |Classification of osmotic pressure = |Number of accommodations = |Total capacity = |Annual bathers = |Year of statistics = |External link = http://www.minakamionsen.com/ |Remarks = }} '''Minakami Onsen''' (水上温泉) is an onsen located in [[:en:Minakami, Gunma|Minakami]], Tone District, Gunma Prefecture. The area south of [[:en:Mount Tanigawa|Mount Tanigawa]] is known as {{ill|Minakami Onsenkyo|ja|水上温泉郷|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minakami Onsenkyo|qq=|1=Minakami Onsenkyō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11548229}}, with Minakami Onsen serving as its central hub. Following [[:en:World War II|World War II]], its excellent {{ill|transportation access|ja|交通アクセス|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/transportation access|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to アクセス|1=Access|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q338902}} facilitated rapid expansion, establishing it as one of the major onsen areas in the prefecture alongside [[:en:Kusatsu Onsen|Kusatsu Onsen]] and {{ill|Ikaho Onsen|ja|伊香保温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikaho Onsen|qq=}}. In the {{ill|Jomo Karuta|ja|上毛かるた|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jomo Karuta|qq=|1=Jōmō Karuta|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17216027}}, it is celebrated as "Mi no fuda: Minakami-Tanigawa, skiing and climbing." == Hot Spring Properties == * Calcium - {{ill|Sulfate spring|ja|硫酸塩泉|1=Sulfate spring|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sulfate spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Sulfate spring|qq=}} == Onsen Town == The onsen town clusters around [[:en:Minakami Station|Minakami Station]] on the [[:en:Jōetsu Line|Jōetsu Line]], with hotels and inns densely packed along the steep banks of the upper [[:en:Tone River|Tone River]]. The area is dotted with scenic spots such as {{ill|Suwa Gorge|ja|諏訪峡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Gorge|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and Minakami Gorge, attracting numerous visitors during the autumn foliage season. The slow-moving {{ill|Horse-drawn carriage|ja|トテ馬車|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Horse-drawn carriage|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 馬車|1=horse-drawn vehicle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1112950}} rides are a signature attraction of Minakami Onsen. Bridges over the Tone River, such as Yuhara Bridge and Minakami Bridge, are known for their nighttime illumination. Traditional amusements, including {{ill|Smart ball|ja|スマートボール|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Smart ball|qq=|1=Smart ball|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11313289}} parlors, evoke nostalgia and draw modern visitors. Despite its focus on overnight stays, day-use facilities are limited but increasing. Many inns now accommodate day-use visitors. Public facilities include the town-run "Yuterume Tanigawa" and the "Fureai Koryukan," which offers foot baths. == History == According to legend, Minakami Onsen was founded during the {{ill|Eiroku era|ja|永禄年間|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eiroku era|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} when the monk Kaiō discovered steam rising from a cliff along the Tone River. Before the opening of the {{ill|Shimizu Tunnel|ja|清水トンネル|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimizu Tunnel|qq=|1=Shimizu Tunnels|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q75220055}}, the area was a remote mountain retreat. Writers like [[:en:Bokusui Wakayama|Bokusui Wakayama]], [[:en:Osamu Dazai|Osamu Dazai]], [[:en:Hakushū Kitahara|Hakushū Kitahara]], and [[:en:Yosano Akiko|Akiko Yosano]] were captivated by the harmony of hot springs and dramatic valleys. The area was then known as Yuhara Onsen, a name still in occasional use. The opening of the [[:en:Jōetsu Line|Jōetsu Line]] in 1931 brought Minakami Onsen closer to the [[:en:Capital region|Greater Tokyo Area]]. The 1966 {{ill|Kikufuji Hotel fire|ja|菊富士ホテル火災|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kikufuji Hotel fire|qq=|1=Kikufuji Hotel fire|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11619620}} marked a significant event in its history. The construction of the [[:en:Kan-etsu Expressway|Kan-Etsu Expressway]] and the introduction of express trains like the {{ill|Yukemuri Express|ja|水上列車|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yukemuri Express|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} led to its rise as a major recreation destination. However, the shift towards group tours and nightlife transformed the onsen into a bustling entertainment hub, complete with bars and show pubs. Following the [[:en:Lost Decades|economic bubble collapse]], declining group tours and changing tourist preferences forced the onsen to refocus. Today, it aims to leverage its natural surroundings, integrating [[:en:Skiing|skiing]], [[:en:Rafting|rafting]], and other outdoor activities to develop as a recreational resort. == Recent Issues == The {{ill|Ōshimizu Tunnel|ja|大清水トンネル|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōshimizu Tunnel|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 清水トンネル|1=Shimizu Tunnels|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q75220055}} of the [[:en:Jōetsu Shinkansen|Jōetsu Shinkansen]] passes 50 meters beneath the hot spring's aquifers, causing decreased water flow. In 1979, the issue was addressed in the [[:en:House of Representatives (Japan)|House of Representatives of Japan]], prompting the [[:en:Japan Railway Construction Public Corporation|Japan Railway Construction Public Corporation]] to drill new wells. The 2004 {{ill|onsen fraud scandal|ja|温泉偽装問題|1=Onsen Fraud Controversy|qq=Onsen Fraud Scandal|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/onsen fraud scandal|13=User:Immanuelle/Onsen Fraud Controversy|qq=}} revealed that some facilities used tap water while advertising natural hot springs, raising concerns over misrepresentation and improper tax collection. The {{ill|abandoned Hotel Ōmiya|ja|ホテル大宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/abandoned Hotel Ōmiya|qq=|1=Abandoned Hotel Ōmiya|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q85873736}}, located near Minakami Station, remains an unresolved issue for the local community. == Access == * Train: [[:en:Jōetsu Line|Jōetsu Line]], Minakami Station, approximately 5 minutes on foot * Car: Exit at Minakami Interchange on the [[:en:Kan-etsu Expressway|Kan-Etsu Expressway]], via National Route 291 == References == {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * ''Disappearing Ruins of Japan: Hidden History Through Ruins,'' Taiyo Publishing, 2014 == See Also == * {{ill|Ii Yudana|ja|いい湯だな|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ii Yudana|qq=|1=Ii Yudana|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11259008}} (song by {{ill|The Dukes Aces|ja|デューク・エイセス|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/The Dukes Aces|qq=|1=Duke Aces|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11320788}}) - Features Minakami in the fourth verse == External Links == * [https://www.enjoy-minakami.jp/ Minakami Perfect Guide] - Official tourism website of Minakami {{Onsen}} {{translated page|ja|水上温泉|version=95165560|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Sulfate spring]] [[Category:Copper-Iron Spring]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] }} [[ja:水上温泉]] [[Category:Hot springs of Gunma Prefecture]] {{Commons category|Minakami Onsen}} {{wikidata link|Q11548228}}

The {{ill|abandoned Hotel Ōmiya|ja|ホテル大宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/abandoned Hotel Ōmiya|qq=|1=Abandoned Hotel Ōmiya|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q85873736}}, located near Minakami Station, remains an unresolved issue for the local community. == Access == * Train: [[:en:Jōetsu Line|Jōetsu Line]], Minakami Station, approximately 5 minutes on foot * Car: Exit at Minakami Interchange on the [[:en:Kan-etsu Expressway|Kan-Etsu Expressway]], via National Route 291 == References == {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * ''Disappearing Ruins of Japan: Hidden History Through Ruins,'' Taiyo Publishing, 2014 == See Also == * {{ill|Ii Yudana|ja|いい湯だな|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ii Yudana|qq=|1=Ii Yudana|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11259008}} (song by {{ill|The Dukes Aces|ja|デューク・エイセス|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/The Dukes Aces|qq=|1=Duke Aces|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11320788}}) - Features Minakami in the fourth verse == External Links == * [https://www.enjoy-minakami.jp/ Minakami Perfect Guide] - Official tourism website of Minakami {{Onsen}} {{translated page|ja|水上温泉|version=95165560|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Sulfate spring]] [[Category:Copper-Iron Spring]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] }} [[ja:水上温泉]] [[Category:Hot springs of Gunma Prefecture]] {{Commons category|Minakami Onsen}} {{wikidata link|Q11548228}}

[[sn:commons:Category:Minakami Onsen]][[ja:水上温泉]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11548228]] Wikidata name: Minakami Onsen [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:11, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Unreferenced|date=March 2017}} {{Onsen in Japan |Name = Minakami Onsen |image = Minakami Onsen 01.jpg |Caption = [[:en:Tone River|Tone River]] riverside view at Minakami-kan |Location = [[:en:Minakami, Gunma|Minakami]], Tone District, Gunma Prefecture | Latitude degrees = 36 | Latitude minutes = 45 | Latitude seconds = 59 | Longitude degrees = 138 | Longitude minutes = 57 | Longitude seconds = 29 |Access = Train: [[:en:Jōetsu Line|Jōetsu Line]], alight at Minakami Station, approximately 5 minutes on foot
Car: Exit at {{ill|Minakami Interchange|ja|水上インターチェンジ|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minakami Interchange|1=Minakami interchange|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11129404}} on [[:en:Kan-etsu Expressway|Kan-Etsu Expressway]], follow National Route 291 |Hot spring type = {{ill|Sulfate spring|ja|硫酸塩泉|1=Sulfate spring|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sulfate spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Sulfate spring|qq=}} |Flow rate = |pH = |Classification of fluid = |Classification of osmotic pressure = |Number of accommodations = |Total capacity = |Annual bathers = |Year of statistics = |External link = http://www.minakamionsen.com/ |Remarks = }} '''Minakami Onsen''' (水上温泉) is an onsen located in [[:en:Minakami, Gunma|Minakami]], Tone District, Gunma Prefecture. The area south of [[:en:Mount Tanigawa|Mount Tanigawa]] is known as {{ill|Minakami Onsenkyo|ja|水上温泉郷|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minakami Onsenkyo|1=Minakami Onsenkyō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11548229}}, with Minakami Onsen serving as its central hub. Following [[:en:World War II|World War II]], its excellent {{ill|transportation access|ja|交通アクセス|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/transportation access|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to アクセス|1=Access|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q338902}} facilitated rapid expansion, establishing it as one of the major onsen areas in the prefecture alongside [[:en:Kusatsu Onsen|Kusatsu Onsen]] and {{ill|Ikaho Onsen|ja|伊香保温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikaho Onsen|qq=}}. In the {{ill|Jomo Karuta|ja|上毛かるた|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jomo Karuta|1=Jōmō Karuta|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17216027}}, it is celebrated as "Mi no fuda: Minakami-Tanigawa, skiing and climbing." == Hot Spring Properties == * Calcium - {{ill|Sulfate spring|ja|硫酸塩泉|1=Sulfate spring|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sulfate spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Sulfate spring|qq=}} == Onsen Town == The onsen town clusters around [[:en:Minakami Station|Minakami Station]] on the [[:en:Jōetsu Line|Jōetsu Line]], with hotels and inns densely packed along the steep banks of the upper [[:en:Tone River|Tone River]]. The area is dotted with scenic spots such as {{ill|Suwa Gorge|ja|諏訪峡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Gorge|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and Minakami Gorge, attracting numerous visitors during the autumn foliage season. The slow-moving {{ill|Horse-drawn carriage|ja|トテ馬車|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Horse-drawn carriage|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 馬車|1=horse-drawn vehicle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1112950}} rides are a signature attraction of Minakami Onsen. Bridges over the Tone River, such as Yuhara Bridge and Minakami Bridge, are known for their nighttime illumination. Traditional amusements, including {{ill|Smart ball|ja|スマートボール|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Smart ball|1=Smart ball|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11313289}} parlors, evoke nostalgia and draw modern visitors. Despite its focus on overnight stays, day-use facilities are limited but increasing. Many inns now accommodate day-use visitors. Public facilities include the town-run "Yuterume Tanigawa" and the "Fureai Koryukan," which offers foot baths. == History == According to legend, Minakami Onsen was founded during the {{ill|Eiroku era|ja|永禄年間|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eiroku era|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} when the monk Kaiō discovered steam rising from a cliff along the Tone River. Before the opening of the {{ill|Shimizu Tunnel|ja|清水トンネル|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimizu Tunnel|1=Shimizu Tunnels|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q75220055}}, the area was a remote mountain retreat. Writers like [[:en:Bokusui Wakayama|Bokusui Wakayama]], [[:en:Osamu Dazai|Osamu Dazai]], [[:en:Hakushū Kitahara|Hakushū Kitahara]], and [[:en:Yosano Akiko|Akiko Yosano]] were captivated by the harmony of hot springs and dramatic valleys. The area was then known as Yuhara Onsen, a name still in occasional use. The opening of the [[:en:Jōetsu Line|Jōetsu Line]] in 1931 brought Minakami Onsen closer to the [[:en:Capital region|Greater Tokyo Area]]. The 1966 {{ill|Kikufuji Hotel fire|ja|菊富士ホテル火災|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kikufuji Hotel fire|1=Kikufuji Hotel fire|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11619620}} marked a significant event in its history. The construction of the [[:en:Kan-etsu Expressway|Kan-Etsu Expressway]] and the introduction of express trains like the {{ill|Yukemuri Express|ja|水上列車|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yukemuri Express|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} led to its rise as a major recreation destination. However, the shift towards group tours and nightlife transformed the onsen into a bustling entertainment hub, complete with bars and show pubs. Following the [[:en:Lost Decades|economic bubble collapse]], declining group tours and changing tourist preferences forced the onsen to refocus. Today, it aims to leverage its natural surroundings, integrating [[:en:Skiing|skiing]], [[:en:Rafting|rafting]], and other outdoor activities to develop as a recreational resort. == Recent Issues == The {{ill|Ōshimizu Tunnel|ja|大清水トンネル|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōshimizu Tunnel|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 清水トンネル|1=Shimizu Tunnels|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q75220055}} of the [[:en:Jōetsu Shinkansen|Jōetsu Shinkansen]] passes 50 meters beneath the hot spring's aquifers, causing decreased water flow. In 1979, the issue was addressed in the [[:en:House of Representatives (Japan)|House of Representatives of Japan]], prompting the [[:en:Japan Railway Construction Public Corporation|Japan Railway Construction Public Corporation]] to drill new wells. The 2004 {{ill|onsen fraud scandal|ja|温泉偽装問題|1=Onsen Fraud Controversy|qq=Onsen Fraud Scandal|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/onsen fraud scandal|13=User:Immanuelle/Onsen Fraud Controversy|qq=}} revealed that some facilities used tap water while advertising natural hot springs, raising concerns over misrepresentation and improper tax collection. The {{ill|abandoned Hotel Ōmiya|ja|ホテル大宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/abandoned Hotel Ōmiya|1=Abandoned Hotel Ōmiya|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q85873736}}, located near Minakami Station, remains an unresolved issue for the local community. == Access == * Train: [[:en:Jōetsu Line|Jōetsu Line]], Minakami Station, approximately 5 minutes on foot * Car: Exit at Minakami Interchange on the [[:en:Kan-etsu Expressway|Kan-Etsu Expressway]], via National Route 291 == References == {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * ''Disappearing Ruins of Japan: Hidden History Through Ruins,'' Taiyo Publishing, 2014 == See Also == * {{ill|Ii Yudana|ja|いい湯だな|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ii Yudana|1=Ii Yudana|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11259008}} (song by {{ill|The Dukes Aces|ja|デューク・エイセス|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/The Dukes Aces|1=Duke Aces|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11320788}}) - Features Minakami in the fourth verse == External Links == * [https://www.enjoy-minakami.jp/ Minakami Perfect Guide] - Official tourism website of Minakami {{Onsen}} {{translated page|ja|水上温泉|version=95165560|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Sulfate spring]] [[Category:Copper-Iron Spring]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] }} [[ja:水上温泉]] [[Category:Hot springs of Gunma Prefecture]] {{Commons category|Minakami Onsen}} {{wikidata link|Q11548228}}

However, the shift towards group tours and nightlife transformed the onsen into a bustling entertainment hub, complete with bars and show pubs. Following the [[:en:Lost Decades|economic bubble collapse]], declining group tours and changing tourist preferences forced the onsen to refocus. Today, it aims to leverage its natural surroundings, integrating [[:en:Skiing|skiing]], [[:en:Rafting|rafting]], and other outdoor activities to develop as a recreational resort. == Recent Issues == The {{ill|Ōshimizu Tunnel|ja|大清水トンネル|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōshimizu Tunnel|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 清水トンネル|1=Shimizu Tunnels|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q75220055}} of the [[:en:Jōetsu Shinkansen|Jōetsu Shinkansen]] passes 50 meters beneath the hot spring's aquifers, causing decreased water flow. In 1979, the issue was addressed in the [[:en:House of Representatives (Japan)|House of Representatives of Japan]], prompting the [[:en:Japan Railway Construction Public Corporation|Japan Railway Construction Public Corporation]] to drill new wells. The 2004 {{ill|onsen fraud scandal|ja|温泉偽装問題|1=Onsen Fraud Controversy|qq=Onsen Fraud Scandal|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/onsen fraud scandal|13=User:Immanuelle/Onsen Fraud Controversy|qq=}} revealed that some facilities used tap water while advertising natural hot springs, raising concerns over misrepresentation and improper tax collection. The {{ill|abandoned Hotel Ōmiya|ja|ホテル大宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/abandoned Hotel Ōmiya|1=Abandoned Hotel Ōmiya|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q85873736}}, located near Minakami Station, remains an unresolved issue for the local community. == Access == * Train: [[:en:Jōetsu Line|Jōetsu Line]], Minakami Station, approximately 5 minutes on foot * Car: Exit at Minakami Interchange on the [[:en:Kan-etsu Expressway|Kan-Etsu Expressway]], via National Route 291 == References == {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * ''Disappearing Ruins of Japan: Hidden History Through Ruins,'' Taiyo Publishing, 2014 == See Also == * {{ill|Ii Yudana|ja|いい湯だな|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ii Yudana|1=Ii Yudana|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11259008}} (song by {{ill|The Dukes Aces|ja|デューク・エイセス|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/The Dukes Aces|1=Duke Aces|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11320788}}) - Features Minakami in the fourth verse == External Links == * [https://www.enjoy-minakami.jp/ Minakami Perfect Guide] - Official tourism website of Minakami {{Onsen}} {{translated page|ja|水上温泉|version=95165560|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Sulfate spring]] [[Category:Copper-Iron Spring]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] }} [[ja:水上温泉]] [[Category:Hot springs of Gunma Prefecture]] {{Commons category|Minakami Onsen}} {{wikidata link|Q11548228}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

[[sn:commons:Category:Minakami Onsen]][[ja:水上温泉]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11548228]] Wikidata name: Minakami Onsen [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:11, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Unreferenced|date=March 2017}} {{Onsen in Japan |Name = Minakami Onsen |image = Minakami Onsen 01.jpg |Caption = [[:en:Tone River|Tone River]] riverside view at Minakami-kan |Location = [[:en:Minakami, Gunma|Minakami]], Tone District, Gunma Prefecture | Latitude degrees = 36 | Latitude minutes = 45 | Latitude seconds = 59 | Longitude degrees = 138 | Longitude minutes = 57 | Longitude seconds = 29 |Access = Train: [[:en:Jōetsu Line|Jōetsu Line]], alight at Minakami Station, approximately 5 minutes on foot
Car: Exit at {{ill|Minakami Interchange|ja|水上インターチェンジ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minakami Interchange|1=Minakami interchange|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11129404}} on [[:en:Kan-etsu Expressway|Kan-Etsu Expressway]], follow National Route 291 |Hot spring type = {{ill|Sulfate spring|ja|硫酸塩泉|1=Sulfate spring|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sulfate spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Sulfate spring|qq=}} |Flow rate = |pH = |Classification of fluid = |Classification of osmotic pressure = |Number of accommodations = |Total capacity = |Annual bathers = |Year of statistics = |External link = http://www.minakamionsen.com/ |Remarks = }} '''Minakami Onsen''' (水上温泉) is an onsen located in [[:en:Minakami, Gunma|Minakami]], Tone District, Gunma Prefecture. The area south of [[:en:Mount Tanigawa|Mount Tanigawa]] is known as {{ill|Minakami Onsenkyo|ja|水上温泉郷|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minakami Onsenkyo|1=Minakami Onsenkyō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11548229}}, with Minakami Onsen serving as its central hub. Following [[:en:World War II|World War II]], its excellent {{ill|transportation access|ja|交通アクセス|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/transportation access|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to アクセス|1=Access|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q338902}} facilitated rapid expansion, establishing it as one of the major onsen areas in the prefecture alongside [[:en:Kusatsu Onsen|Kusatsu Onsen]] and {{ill|Ikaho Onsen|ja|伊香保温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikaho Onsen|qq=}}. In the {{ill|Jomo Karuta|ja|上毛かるた|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jomo Karuta|1=Jōmō Karuta|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17216027}}, it is celebrated as "Mi no fuda: Minakami-Tanigawa, skiing and climbing." == Hot Spring Properties == * Calcium - {{ill|Sulfate spring|ja|硫酸塩泉|1=Sulfate spring|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sulfate spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Sulfate spring|qq=}} == Onsen Town == The onsen town clusters around [[:en:Minakami Station|Minakami Station]] on the [[:en:Jōetsu Line|Jōetsu Line]], with hotels and inns densely packed along the steep banks of the upper [[:en:Tone River|Tone River]]. The area is dotted with scenic spots such as {{ill|Suwa Gorge|ja|諏訪峡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Gorge|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and Minakami Gorge, attracting numerous visitors during the autumn foliage season. The slow-moving {{ill|Horse-drawn carriage|ja|トテ馬車|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Horse-drawn carriage|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 馬車|1=horse-drawn vehicle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1112950}} rides are a signature attraction of Minakami Onsen. Bridges over the Tone River, such as Yuhara Bridge and Minakami Bridge, are known for their nighttime illumination. Traditional amusements, including {{ill|Smart ball|ja|スマートボール|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Smart ball|1=Smart ball|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11313289}} parlors, evoke nostalgia and draw modern visitors. Despite its focus on overnight stays, day-use facilities are limited but increasing. Many inns now accommodate day-use visitors. Public facilities include the town-run "Yuterume Tanigawa" and the "Fureai Koryukan," which offers foot baths. == History == According to legend, Minakami Onsen was founded during the {{ill|Eiroku era|ja|永禄年間|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eiroku era|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} when the monk Kaiō discovered steam rising from a cliff along the Tone River. Before the opening of the {{ill|Shimizu Tunnel|ja|清水トンネル|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimizu Tunnel|1=Shimizu Tunnels|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q75220055}}, the area was a remote mountain retreat. Writers like [[:en:Bokusui Wakayama|Bokusui Wakayama]], [[:en:Osamu Dazai|Osamu Dazai]], [[:en:Hakushū Kitahara|Hakushū Kitahara]], and [[:en:Yosano Akiko|Akiko Yosano]] were captivated by the harmony of hot springs and dramatic valleys. The area was then known as Yuhara Onsen, a name still in occasional use. The opening of the [[:en:Jōetsu Line|Jōetsu Line]] in 1931 brought Minakami Onsen closer to the [[:en:Capital region|Greater Tokyo Area]]. The 1966 {{ill|Kikufuji Hotel fire|ja|菊富士ホテル火災|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kikufuji Hotel fire|1=Kikufuji Hotel fire|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11619620}} marked a significant event in its history. The construction of the [[:en:Kan-etsu Expressway|Kan-Etsu Expressway]] and the introduction of express trains like the {{ill|Yukemuri Express|ja|水上列車|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yukemuri Express|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} led to its rise as a major recreation destination. However, the shift towards group tours and nightlife transformed the onsen into a bustling entertainment hub, complete with bars and show pubs. Following the [[:en:Lost Decades|economic bubble collapse]], declining group tours and changing tourist preferences forced the onsen to refocus. Today, it aims to leverage its natural surroundings, integrating [[:en:Skiing|skiing]], [[:en:Rafting|rafting]], and other outdoor activities to develop as a recreational resort. == Recent Issues == The {{ill|Ōshimizu Tunnel|ja|大清水トンネル|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōshimizu Tunnel|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 清水トンネル|1=Shimizu Tunnels|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q75220055}} of the [[:en:Jōetsu Shinkansen|Jōetsu Shinkansen]] passes 50 meters beneath the hot spring's aquifers, causing decreased water flow. In 1979, the issue was addressed in the [[:en:House of Representatives (Japan)|House of Representatives of Japan]], prompting the [[:en:Japan Railway Construction Public Corporation|Japan Railway Construction Public Corporation]] to drill new wells. The 2004 {{ill|onsen fraud scandal|ja|温泉偽装問題|1=Onsen Fraud Controversy|qq=Onsen Fraud Scandal|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/onsen fraud scandal|13=User:Immanuelle/Onsen Fraud Controversy|qq=}} revealed that some facilities used tap water while advertising natural hot springs, raising concerns over misrepresentation and improper tax collection. The {{ill|abandoned Hotel Ōmiya|ja|ホテル大宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/abandoned Hotel Ōmiya|1=Abandoned Hotel Ōmiya|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q85873736}}, located near Minakami Station, remains an unresolved issue for the local community. == Access == * Train: [[:en:Jōetsu Line|Jōetsu Line]], Minakami Station, approximately 5 minutes on foot * Car: Exit at Minakami Interchange on the [[:en:Kan-etsu Expressway|Kan-Etsu Expressway]], via National Route 291 == References == {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * ''Disappearing Ruins of Japan: Hidden History Through Ruins,'' Taiyo Publishing, 2014 == See Also == * {{ill|Ii Yudana|ja|いい湯だな|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ii Yudana|1=Ii Yudana|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11259008}} (song by {{ill|The Dukes Aces|ja|デューク・エイセス|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/The Dukes Aces|1=Duke Aces|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11320788}}) - Features Minakami in the fourth verse == External Links == * [https://www.enjoy-minakami.jp/ Minakami Perfect Guide] - Official tourism website of Minakami {{Onsen}} {{translated page|ja|水上温泉|version=95165560|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Sulfate spring]] [[Category:Copper-Iron Spring]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] }} [[ja:水上温泉]] [[Category:Hot springs of Gunma Prefecture]] {{Commons category|Minakami Onsen}} {{wikidata link|Q11548228}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]]

The opening of the [[:en:Jōetsu Line|Jōetsu Line]] in 1931 brought Minakami Onsen closer to the [[:en:Capital region|Greater Tokyo Area]].
